title: "Connection refused" using rootful podman and trying to access the host via `host.containers.internal`
body:
### Issue Description

I spawn a service ([Segger J-Link Remote Server](https://www.segger.com/products/debug-probes/j-link/tools/j-link-remote-server/)) listening on port 19020 on my host, which is a Windows 11 (24H2) machine.

That service should be accessible by podman containers. For root**less** containers, there seem to be multiple ways, e.g. using pasta, or `host.docker.internal`, see https://github.com/eriksjolund/podman-networking-docs?tab=readme-ov-file#outbound-tcpudp-connections-to-the-hosts-localhost. But how does this work for rootful containers? https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/main/docs/tutorials/basic_networking.md does not tell me or I don't understand.

As far as I understand, the default network for rootful containers uses a bridged network, so there must be a route created somewhere in the NAT. Is that implemented or not yet?

### Steps to reproduce the issue

1. spin up a service listening on a port on the host, e.g. `& "C:\Program Files\SEGGER\JLink_V882\JLinkRemoteServer.exe"` which listens on 19020
2. spin up podman container: 
```
podman run -it --rm busybox telnet localhost 19020
telnet: can't connect to remote host (127.0.0.1): Connection refused
```
3. same for "magic url":
```
podman run -it --rm busybox telnet host.containers.internal 19020
telnet: can't connect to remote host (10.88.0.1): Connection refused
```
4. using host network works, but causes other issues - I need to use the bridged version
```
podman run -it --rm --network=host busybox telnet localhost 19020
Connected to localhost
)�CSEGGER J-Link Remote Server V8.82 compiled 11:49:35 on Oct 29 2025
```
5. magic url does not work with host network either (guess that makes sense):
```
podman run -it --rm --network=host busybox telnet host.containers.internal 19020
telnet: can't connect to remote host (10.255.255.254): Connection refused
```

### Describe the results you received

Connection refused.

### Describe the results you expected

An established connection to the Segger service, e.g., `)�CSEGGER J-Link Remote Server V8.82 compiled 11:49:35 on Oct 29 2025` response

### podman info output

### Podman in a container

No

### Privileged Or Rootless

Privileged

### Upstream Latest Release

Yes

### Additional environment details

I installed podman via Podman Desktop, but I believe this is an underlying podman issue, not a Podman Desktop issue, correct?

There are in- and outbound firewall rules in place on the host that allow port 19020 connections.


### Additional information

Thanks for any input.

comments.body: A friendly reminder that this issue had no activity for 30 days.

Am I the only one with this request? 😁

I'd love to access services on the host without having to use --network=host. 

Is that possible or not at the moment?

localhost in container is a different host (in a different namespace) than localhost on host (unless you specify `--network=host`) - it shouldn't work, that's not a bug, it's a feature

`host.containers.internal` resolves to address that is owned by the host. It works as expected on linux machine:

```
❯ python3 -m http.server
Serving HTTP on 0.0.0.0 port 8000 (http://0.0.0.0:8000/) ...
10.88.0.9 - - [21/Feb/2026 20:37:51] "GET / HTTP/1.1" 200 -
```

```
❯ podman run --rm -it alpine wget host.containers.internal:8000
Connecting to host.containers.internal:8000 (10.88.0.1:8000)
saving to 'index.html'
index.html           100% |******************************************************************************************************|  1060  0:00:00 ETA
'index.html' saved
```

Podman doesn't work on Windows natively, it uses WSL2, which is a kind of Linux VM. I guess that's where the issue is. `host.containers.internal` point to address owned by the Linux VM host. Linux doesn't run any service on port 19020 and is not aware that Windows does, so it responds with "Connection refused". 

You will need to use address of the Windows host (I have no experience with WSL so I will be guessing):

- `host.wsl.internal`
- IP of adapter called "vEthernet (WSL)"
- https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/windows/wsl/networking#accessing-windows-networking-apps-from-linux-host-ip
- any address of windows network interface (non overlapping with Linux)

or try to play with that fishy feature... thou, connection working with `--network=host` makes me suspect that you've already did:
- https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/windows/wsl/networking#mirrored-mode-networking

> localhost in container is a different host (in a different namespace) than localhost on host (unless you specify `--network=host`) - it shouldn't work, that's not a bug, it's a feature

I see that, which is why I wanted to use `host.containers.internal`. It's much clearer than using localhost, but as I wrote in my first post, it does not work.
> 
> `host.containers.internal` resolves to address that is owned by the host. It works as expected on linux machine:
> 

Okay, but it does not on Windows.

> Podman doesn't work on Windows natively, it uses WSL2, which is a kind of Linux VM. I guess that's where the issue is. `host.containers.internal` point to address owned by the Linux VM host. Linux doesn't run any service on port 19020 and is not aware that Windows does, so it responds with "Connection refused".
> 

Quite likely, yes.

> You will need to use address of the Windows host (I have no experience with WSL so I will be guessing):
> 
> * `host.wsl.internal`

```
podman run -it --rm busybox telnet host.wsl.internal 19020
telnet: bad address 'host.wsl.internal'
```

> * IP of adapter called "vEthernet (WSL)"

I think that one does not exist anymore when using mirrored mode.

> * https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/windows/wsl/networking#accessing-windows-networking-apps-from-linux-host-ip

```
# in WSL:
ip route show | grep -i default | awk '{ print $3}'
10.126.64.1

# in container
/ # telnet 10.126.64.1 19020
# timeout
```
Note this does not even work from inside WSL (also timeout), but connecting to localhost works (due to mirroring mode)

> * any address of windows network interface (non overlapping with Linux)

```
/ # telnet 192.168.178.38 19020
telnet: can't connect to remote host (192.168.178.38): Connection refused
/ # ping 192.168.178.38
PING 192.168.178.38 (192.168.178.38): 56 data bytes
64 bytes from 192.168.178.38: seq=0 ttl=64 time=0.069 ms
64 bytes from 192.168.178.38: seq=1 ttl=64 time=0.164 ms
^C
--- 192.168.178.38 ping statistics ---
2 packets transmitted, 2 packets received, 0% packet loss
round-trip min/avg/max = 0.069/0.116/0.164 ms
```

> 
> or try to play with that fishy feature... thou, connection working with `--network=host` makes me suspect that you've already did:
> 
> * https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/windows/wsl/networking#mirrored-mode-networking

Why fishy? But yes, I'm using mirrored mode networking which solves issues with DNS and proxies in my environment. I think it's made for my use-case, because the docs say

> Connect to Windows servers from within Linux using the localhost address 127.0.0.1. IPv6 localhost address ::1 is not supported

And this is what I'm trying to do. If `host.containers.internal` resolves in the WSL host IP, I thought, accessing the Windows IP/Service would work, too due to mirror mode. 

But apparently it does not, maybe another route or something is required.

Looks like currently, `--network=host` is the only solution on Windows :(

I've done some testing and experiments I've set up Podman Desktop with WSL2 Backend, on Windows I used python3 http server listening on port 0.0.0.0:8000 ## 1.Mirroring disabled - Windows interfaces: - Ethernet 10.0.100.30 (LAN) - vEthernet 172.30.208.1 - WSL Linux interfaces: - lo 127.0.0.1, 10.255.255.254 - eth0 172.30.220.52 Accessing from WSL VM: `curl 127.0.0.1:8000` - fail `curl 10.0.100.30:8000` - ok `curl 172.30.208.1:8000` - ok Accessing from a podman container container root-less: `podman run --rm alpine/curl -fsSL 127.0.0.1:8000` - fail `podman run --rm alpine/curl -fsSL 10.0.100.30:8000` - works `podman run --rm alpine/curl -fsSL 172.30.208.1:8000` - works Rootful: `sudo podman run --rm alpine/curl -fsSL 127.0.0.1:8000` - fail `sudo podman run --rm alpine/curl -fsSL 10.0.100.30:8000` - works `sudo podman run --rm alpine/curl -fsSL 172.30.208.1:8000` - works #### Conclusion Works as expected. ## 2.Mirroring enabled with: `notepad $env:USERPROFILE\.wslconfig` ``` [wsl2] networkingMode=mirrored dnsTunneling=true firewall=true autoProxy=true ``` then: `wsl --shutdown` and `wsl`, then windows rebooted, vm recreated - Windows interfaces: - Ethernet 10.0.100.30 (LAN) - WSL Linux interfaces: - lo 127.0.0.1, 10.255.255.254 - eth0 10.0.100.30 - loopback0 no-ip-address `curl 10.0.100.30:8000` - fail `curl 127.0.0.1:8000` - success #### Conclusion - There is no classic way to reach Windows as interfaces are mirrored and there is no distinctive address - I tried searching for possibility of adding an interface that won't be mirrored, but did not find anything - loopback0 is shared by some trickery, seems like it is the only way to reach Windows from Linux. - I tried forwarding packets from container to 127.0.0.1 to utilize loopback0: ```bash sysctl -w net.ipv4.conf.podman0.route_localnet=1 nft add table ip nat nft add chain nat prerouting { type nat hook prerouting priority dstnat \; policy accept \; } nft add chain nat postrouting { type nat hook postrouting priority srcnat \; policy accept \; } nft add rule nat prerouting iifname "podman0" tcp dport 8000 dnat to 127.0.0.1:8000 counter # Add SNAT rule as packets with non 127.0.0.1 address on windows loopback are ignored nft add rule nat postrouting oifname "loopback0" ip daddr 127.0.0.1 snat to 127.0.0.1 counter ``` The success is limited. It works correctly once every X times. Most often SYN is received on Windows, SYN ACK response is sent back (see the screenshot from wireshark on windows), but SYN ACK is not seen on linux loopback0. I don't see any pattern in that.
